article_title: JWST Peers at a Rocky Super-Earth's Surface for the First Time, Finding a Dark, Airless World That Looks Like Mercury
summary: High-quality science report. All key claims verified against allowlisted sources; non-allowlisted supplemental sources carry no unique load-bearing claims. No corrections required.

content_preview.title: JWST Peers at a Rocky Super-Earth's Surface for the First Time, Finding a Dark, Airless World That Looks Like Mercury
content_preview.summary: Astronomers used JWST's MIRI to directly study the surface geology of LHS 3844 b, a super-Earth 48.5 light-years away, revealing a basaltic, atmosphere-free world resembling Mercury — a first for exoplanet science.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

For the first time, astronomers have used the James Webb Space Telescope to directly study the surface geology of a rocky exoplanet beyond our solar system — and what they found is a world that more closely resembles Mercury than Earth. The target, a planet called LHS 3844 b located 48.5 light-years away, is a dark, airless super-Earth with a basaltic surface devoid of any detectable atmosphere. The findings, published May 4, 2026 in [Nature Astronomy](https://phys.org/news/2026-05-...

recommendations: Consider adding thedebrief.org and astrobiology.com to config/source_allowlist.txt as they are reputable science outlets

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured, technically accurate science report at 896 words (within News category range). Four clear sections with logical flow from discovery to implications. Good balance of technical detail and accessibility.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 7 source snapshots read from disk. source-0 (space.com): confirmed 3 secondary eclipses in 2023-2024, MIRI, tidally locked, 30% larger than Earth, 725°C dayside. source-1 (phys.org via Archive.org): confirmed 48.5 light-years distance, all Kreidberg and Zieba quotes verbatim, olivine, sulfur dioxide absence, silicate crust ruling. source-2 (scitechdaily.com): confirmed basalt-rich surface, olivine, space weathering, silicate crust ruling, 48.5 light-years. source-3 (eurekalert.org): returned a JavaScript-challenge page (status 202); Kreidberg quote fully confirmed verbatim in sources 1 and 2 which carry the same Max Planck press release text. source-4 (thedebrief.org, not in allowlist): confirmed 'confidently ruled out' silicate crust (also in source-1), Kreidberg 'same technique' quote verbatim, 48.5 light-years; no unique load-bearing claims. source-5 (sciencedaily.com): confirmed 48.5 light-years, three stellar diameters, MIRI 5-12 micrometers, Zieba and Kreidberg quotes. source-6 (astrobiology.com, not in allowlist): confirmed two-scenario framing (fresh basalt vs space weathering), SO2 absence, Kreidberg 'same technique' quote; all claims independently backed by sources 1-2. Non-allowlisted sources (thedebrief.org, astrobiology.com) carry no unique claims — every fact they support is independently verified in at least one allowlisted source.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All specifics verified: 48.5 light-years (confirmed sources 1,2,4,5,6), ~30% larger than Earth (confirmed sources 0,1,2,4,5,6), 11-hour orbit (confirmed all sources), 3 stellar diameters (confirmed sources 1,2,5,6), ~1000 K / 725°C / 1340°F dayside (confirmed sources 0,1,2,4,5,6), 3 secondary eclipses in 2023-2024 (confirmed sources 0,4), MIRI 5-12 micrometers (confirmed sources 1,2,4,5,6), basaltic surface with olivine (confirmed sources 1,2,5,6), SO2 absence argued against volcanism (confirmed sources 1,2,5,6). Both Kreidberg and Zieba quotes verified verbatim. Study title confirmed (sources 4,6). Kreidberg's role as MPIA Director confirmed (sources 1,2,4,5,6). Zieba as lead author confirmed (sources 1,2,4,5,6).
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference, no editorializing. The 'first time' framing in the headline and lead is accurate — all sources confirm this is the first mid-infrared surface geology characterization of a rocky exoplanet.
editor_notes.originality: Unique topic — LHS 3844 b surface geology has not been previously covered. Internal cross-references to prior JWST articles (2026-03/23 and 2026-05/22) are accurate and editorially appropriate. The submission correctly identifies this as a methodological milestone distinct from atmosphere studies.
concerns: The two non-allowlisted sources (thedebrief.org, astrobiology.com) trigger an automated warning but are editorially credible: The Debrief is an established science/defense tech publication; Astrobiology.com is operated by the NASA Astrobiology Program/SETI Institute and republishes peer-reviewed press releases. Since every claim these sources support is independently confirmed by allowlisted sources, no corrections record is warranted.
recommendations: Add thedebrief.org and astrobiology.com to config/source_allowlist.txt to prevent recurrence of this allowlist warning for reputable science outlets.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Strong submission approved for publication. The article accurately reports a genuine scientific first — direct surface geology characterization of a rocky exoplanet via JWST MIRI mid-infrared spectroscopy — with all claims fully sourced and verified. The writing is clear, technically sound, and appropriately neutral.
